output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.9, "R1.5": 0.5, "R1.6": 0.6, "R1.7": 0.7}}

Summary: Critique of the ClariNMT intro/abstract — the framing is clear, but the novelty claim is overstated, the evaluation is thin, and several causal claims outrun the reported evidence.

Substantive issues:

1. The novelty claim is too strong and probably wrong as stated. "First translation framework to use self-generated multi-aspect clarification context" and "first to demonstrate that prompting an LLM to externalize candidate senses before decoding yields measurable gains" both need qualification. There is a sizeable line of work that is materially adjacent: chain-of-thought / "think-before-translate" prompting for MT (e.g., Peng et al. 2023 on GPT-4 translation prompting; He et al. 2023 "Exploring Human-Like Translation Strategy with LLMs" which explicitly prompts for keyword/topic/example before translating); MAPS (He et al.) in particular extracts topic and keyword cues from the source and conditions translation on them, which is structurally the same recipe. Dictionary-/sense-augmented NMT (e.g., Liu et al. on lexically constrained decoding, and Pu & Sennrich on WSD in NMT) covers the "externalize candidate senses" claim from the pre-LLM era. Either narrow the claim ("first to combine paraphrase + contrastive glosses + topic in a single self-generated preamble") or drop the "first" framing and reposition as an empirical study of which clarification signals matter.

2. The evidence does not support the headline framing that ClariNMT resolves ambiguity. The reported gains (1.8 / 1.4 COMET) are on a polysemy-stratified subset, but you do not report (a) gains on a non-polysemous control subset, (b) a targeted lexical-accuracy metric (e.g., contrastive evaluation à la ContraWSD/MuCoW, or accuracy on the specifically ambiguous nouns), or (c) whether the improvement is concentrated on the ambiguous tokens versus diffuse fluency gains from longer prompts. Without at least one of these, the causal story ("works because it disambiguates") is unsupported — the same delta could come from generic prompt-elaboration effects. This is the single biggest weakness.

3. Baseline selection is weak for the claim being made. GPT-3.5 zero-shot with a "standard" prompt is a soft baseline; the natural comparisons are (i) GPT-3.5 with a matched-length but ambiguity-agnostic CoT/elaboration prompt (to isolate the disambiguation contribution from the elaboration contribution), and (ii) MAPS or a similar published prompting pipeline. Matching NLLB-200 "within 0.3 COMET" is also weakly stated — within 0.3 in which direction, and is the gap significant? Report signed deltas and significance.

4. Several quantitative claims need scaffolding before they can be read. "1.8 points" COMET — over what variance? No confidence intervals, no n for the stratified subset, no significance test. "62% preference" on 200 segments — no inter-annotator agreement, no tie rate, no annotator description, no significance vs. 50%. With n=200 and a 62/38 split (ignoring ties), this is significant, but the abstract should still say so explicitly.

5. The ablation summary contradicts the framing. You position contrastive sense-glosses as the central mechanism ("externalize candidate senses before decoding"), but then report that the paraphrase signal contributes the largest gain and glosses are secondary. If paraphrase is doing most of the work, the mechanism is plausibly source-side reformulation / self-consistency rather than sense disambiguation — which again undermines claim 2 above. Either revise the framing to lead with paraphrase-as-clarification, or explain why glosses matter despite contributing less.

6. Methodology gaps that should appear even in an abstract-length pitch: how are "candidate ambiguous nouns" identified at inference time (BabelNet lookup? LLM self-identification?), and is the same resource used at eval as at inference (which would be circular)? Also: is the two-stage call one model call or two, and what is the latency / token-cost overhead vs. baseline? A "lightweight, training-free recipe" claim needs at least a one-line cost statement.

7. Scope of the two languages is narrow for the generality claim. zh-en and en-de share little typologically with, say, low-resource or morphologically rich targets where lexical-choice errors look different. Either soften "deploying LLM translation in ambiguity-sensitive settings" or note the language scope as a limitation.

Smaller prose / structure points:

- "Real-world translation queries — particularly in conversational and search settings — frequently contain polysemous tokens whose target rendering depends on user intent." The user-intent framing is asserted but never re-engaged; the method does not actually model user intent, it just elaborates the source. Either drop "user intent" or show how the paraphrase recovers it.
- "Standard zero-shot LLM translation provides no mechanism for surfacing this intent, leading to systematically degraded output." "Systematically degraded" is strong without a citation or pointer to a figure.
- The two-stage description mixes spec and prose. Consider moving the (i)/(ii)/(iii) enumeration into a compact pseudocode or pipeline figure reference, and using the paragraph for motivation.
- "Matches NLLB-200 within 0.3 COMET ... despite using no MT-specific supervision" — GPT-3.5's pretraining is not "no MT-specific supervision" in any strict sense; it has seen vast parallel and quasi-parallel data. Rephrase as "no task-specific fine-tuning."
- The final paragraph re-states the contribution and the deployment pitch; one of these can be cut.

Recommended priority order for revision: (1) fix the novelty claim against MAPS / He et al. 2023 and the WSD-in-NMT literature; (2) add a non-polysemous control and a lexical-accuracy metric, or reframe the mechanism; (3) add a matched-prompt-length baseline; (4) add CIs / significance to the headline numbers.

Uncertainty: I have not verified the exact titles/years/claims of Peng et al. 2023, He et al. 2023 (MAPS), Pu & Sennrich, or the BabelNet polysemy-filter convention against current literature in this turn — treat those as pointers to check, not as established citations. The significance estimate for 62/200 is back-of-envelope.
